perturbation.original_text: Recipient will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon request, together with any copies, except as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon request, together with any copies. This clause is void where it contradicts applicable state or federal law.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'except as otherwise required by law' clause to an earlier sentence negates the later one. This makes the 'except' provision unclear regarding its application to returning or destroying CI, which is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misuse of trade secrets. By implying the recipient can return or destroy under most circumstances, then using the clause stating it is void in cases of contradiction, the new modified text could be interpreted as a permission of misappropriation when injunctive relief under CUTSA would be a more appropriate remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.3 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.3. (a) A complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (b) If neither damages nor unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation are provable, the court may order payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subdivision (a) or (b). (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by potentially allowing the recipient to return or destroy tangible confidential information (CI) even when injunctive relief under CUTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) would be more appropriate. The original text had an exception 'except as otherwise required by law'. The changed text moves this exception, making it unclear if it applies to the return or destruction of CI, which is problematic. The LLM Law Explanation suggests this could be interpreted as permission of misappropriation where injunctive relief would be a more appropriate remedy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426.3, discusses remedies for misappropriation, including damages. By creating ambiguity around the obligation to return or destroy CI when the law might require something else (like preventing the use of the trade secret through an injunction), the modified text undermines the protection offered by CUTSA, potentially leading to its violation. The modified clause could fail legal scrutiny because it does not ensure compliance with the legal requirements of CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA contains the complete agreement between the parties as to the subject matter, and replaces and supersedes any other communications, or agreements, whether oral or written concerning the subje ct matter. Regardless of conflict of laws principles, this NDA is governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws in force in the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA contains the complete agreement between the parties as to the subject matter, and replaces and supersedes any other communications, or agreements, whether oral or written concerning the subje ct matter. This NDA is governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws in force in the State of California, but the jurisdiction for disputes will be in Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The clause 'the jurisdiction for disputes will be in Delaware' is added. This contradicts the clause that states the NDA is governed and construed in accordance with California law, creating a legal contradiction. Choice of law and choice of forum are distinct concepts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California law allows parties to agree to jurisdiction elsewhere, adding it at the end like this, after saying California law applies, creates ambiguity and potential conflict. It is possible for parties to agree in a contract that one state's laws apply, and another state's courts will decide the dispute (or that there is an alternative dispute resolution method, such as arbitration), this is less about if this is permitted under the law, and more about creating a contradiction through the construction of the contract itself.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.40 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.40. Any person may maintain an action or proceeding in a court of this state against a foreign corporation or nonresident person where the action or proceeding arises out of or relates to any contract, agreement, or undertaking for which a choice of California law has been made in whole or in part by the parties thereto and which (a) is a contract, agreement, or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, relating to a transaction involving in the aggregate not less than one million dollars ($1,000,000), and (b) contains a provision or provisions under which the foreign corporation or nonresident agrees to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state. This section applies to contracts, agreements, and undertakings entered into before, on, or after its effective date; it shall be fully retroactive. Contracts, agreements, and undertakings selecting Californi
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential legal contradiction by specifying Delaware as the jurisdiction for disputes while simultaneously stating that California law governs the NDA. The Law Explanation states this "creates ambiguity and potential conflict" because it introduces a separate jurisdiction after stating California law applies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses choice of law, and reinforces that parties can agree to California law. The original text specified California law. The modified text adds a conflicting jurisdiction. While not explicitly illegal to have different choice of law and jurisdiction, this structure creates a potential conflict and ambiguity, which counts as a legal contradiction under 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this NDA upon 10 days ’ written notice . However, all CI provided under this NDA will remain subject to the terms of this NDA.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this NDA upon 10 days ’ written notice. This NDA will terminate without recourse after 30 days regardless of any CI provided.
perturbation.explanation: The original language notes that 'all CI provided under this NDA will remain subject to the terms of this NDA', whereas the modified language states that this NDA will terminate in 30 days regardless of the CI provided. This introduces a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California law allows for modification of contracts, the altered terms create internal inconsistencies regarding the obligations concerning confidential information after the NDA terminates, making the provisions unenforceable and contradictory to established case law requiring clarity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The original text states that CI remains subject to the NDA's terms even after termination, but the changed text terminates the NDA after 30 days regardless of CI, creating a direct contradiction. The provided snippet from California Civil Code 1698(a) states "A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing.", which means the contract can be modified. However, the Law Explanation notes the modification creates internal inconsistencies regarding obligations concerning confidential information, making the provisions unenforceable. Thus the change introduces a significant contradiction regarding the handling of confidential information post-termination, undermining the original obligation. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction, since the change affects the clarity and enforceability of the contract's confidentiality clause.

perturbation.original_text: All CI is provided “AS IS” and all warranties, express, implied, or otherwise are disclaimed. Any attempt to transfer all or part of a party’s rights or obligations under this NDA without the prior written consent of the other party will be null and void.
perturbation.changed_text: All CI is provided “AS IS” and all warranties, express, implied, or otherwise are disclaimed. Any party may assign their rights or obligations under this NDA to a subsidiary.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract states that any attempt to transfer all or part of a party's rights or obligations under this NDA without the prior written consent of the other party will be null and void. The changed text allows a party to assign those same rights or obligations. This causes a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Commercial Code addresses assignment of rights, the direct contradiction of the non-assignment clause within the same document creates ambiguity and legal uncertainty, making the assignment provision questionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2210 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2210. (1) A party may perform his or her duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his or her original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract. No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Except as otherwise provided in Section 9406, unless otherwise agreed, all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him or her by his or her contract, or impair materially his or her chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor’s due performance of his or her entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (3) The creation, attachment, perfection, or enforcement of a security interest in the seller’s interest under a contract is not a transfer that materially changes the duty of, o
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits assignment of rights or obligations without prior written consent. The changed text directly contradicts this by allowing assignment to a subsidiary, creating a direct conflict within the contract. The Law Explanation confirms that this contradiction introduces ambiguity and legal uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2210, states that a party may perform their duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed. However, in this case, it WAS 'otherwise agreed' in the original text, which forbade assignment without consent, and this agreement has been directly violated. The modification introduces a structural flaw (legal contradiction), as defined in the instructions.
